title: It doesn’t have to be us versus nature | Aeon Essays
gist: Ecologist Yadvinder Malhi previews the Nature Relationship Index (NRI), a new country ranking the UN will report annually from late 2026 to complement the Human Development Index by measuring how well nations sustain the living systems their prosperity depends on. The metric scores three dimensions — nature thriving and accessible, nature treated with care (including outsourced consumption footprints), and nature safeguarded for the future — and is designed to penalize wealthy economies whose prosperity depends on destructive supply chains or displaced environmental damage.

insights.claim: A new country ranking called the Nature Relationship Index, proposed in a 2024 Nature paper led by Erle Ellis with the UN Human Development Report Office, will be reported annually by the UN starting in late 2026.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: A UN-branded annual index becomes a new reputational benchmark nations and ESG investors will be measured against from late 2026; track its first release for sovereign and sector signaling.
insights.quote: we proposed a new ranking of countries called the ‘Nature Relationship Index’, which the UN will report annually, starting in late 2026

insights.claim: The Nature Relationship Index scores each country on three dimensions: whether nature is thriving and accessible to people, whether nature is treated with care (low climate and biodiversity impact), and whether nature is safeguarded for the future (protected area, clean rivers and air).
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Defines the explicit scoring axes any country or corporate footprint will be judged on; useful for anticipating which jurisdictions screen well or poorly.
insights.quote: The emerging framework that we proposed last year and are now solidifying in Hangzhou centres on three core dimensions.

insights.claim: The Nature Relationship Index is designed to use spatially explicit consumption-supply-chain data so that a country's embodied carbon emissions and habitat loss are attributed to its consumption, not just its local territory.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Consumption-based (not territorial) accounting reshapes who looks responsible for deforestation and emissions — flags import-dependent rich economies and commodity exporters.
insights.quote: using the latest sophisticated data on long and entangled consumption supply chains and how they embody carbon emissions and natural habitat loss

insights.claim: The index is explicitly designed not to reward poverty or state collapse: a collapsing nation with surging poverty may have a low ecological footprint but should not score as a healthy human-nature relationship.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Anticipates the obvious gaming critique; means the metric aims to distinguish flourishing-through-nature from mere underdevelopment.
insights.quote: We also did not want a metric that appears to reward poverty and state dysfunction as a ‘healthy’ human-nature relationship

insights.claim: Early NRI results suggest the ranking does not simply mirror wealth or Human Development Index position and may produce unexpected outcomes, with high-income high-consumption countries ranking lower when biodiversity loss is outsourced through trade.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: If wealthy nations rank surprisingly low at launch, expect political pushback and headline risk for export-led and import-dependent economies.
insights.quote: It does not simply mirror wealth, and position on the Human Development Index.

insights.claim: Despite abundant local nature, Norway and Canada are flagged as likely to score poorly on a nature-relationship metric because their prosperity relies heavily on fossil-fuel extraction and exports.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Resource-export economies face reputational downgrade under consumption/care-based scoring even where domestic landscapes are pristine.
insights.quote: Think of Norway or Canada, both heavily reliant on fossil-fuel extraction and exports, despite abundant local nature.
insights.locator: On the other hand, a wealthy country...

insights.claim: Brazil is cited as having a large domestic consumption footprint, with a high-meat diet fed by cattle ranches that encroach on its savannas and rainforests.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Domestic (not just export) demand drives Brazilian land-use change — relevant for beef, soy and land-linked deforestation exposure.
insights.quote: Brazil, for example, has a large domestic consumption footprint; its high-meat diet is fed by cattle ranches that eat into its savannas and rainforests.

insights.claim: Much of Europe carries a displaced consumption footprint, meaning its consumption decisions degrade landscapes and seascapes in remote parts of the planet rather than at home.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Consumption-based accounting reassigns hidden environmental liability to wealthy importing blocs — a regulatory and supply-chain disclosure vector.
insights.quote: Others, like much of Europe, have displaced consumption footprints, with decisions on what to consume affecting landscapes and seascapes in remote parts of the planet.

insights.claim: Prosperous cities like Tokyo are flagged as potentially scoring poorly because their urban environments are largely detached from accessible nature.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Urban green-access becomes a scored development quality, putting dense concrete-heavy megacities at a disadvantage.
insights.quote: Or prosperous cities with urban environments largely detached from accessible nature, like Tokyo.
insights.locator: On the other hand, a wealthy country...

themes.theme: Why metrics drive behaviour (the HDI precedent)

insights.claim: The Human Development Index, introduced by the UN in 1990, succeeded in weakening GDP's monopoly on policy imagination by combining income with health and education and publishing an annual peer-comparison benchmark.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: The HDI is the explicit playbook for the NRI; its diffusion pattern (annual ranking → peer pressure → institutional adoption) is what the NRI is betting on.
insights.quote: The Human Development Index helped weaken the monopoly of GDP on the imagination.
insights.locator: Some of the most influential policy metrics...

insights.claim: In the most recent (2023) Human Development Index dataset the US had fallen from first place in 1990 to 17th, with Iceland, Norway and Switzerland at the top, China risen to Western levels, and India at 130 of 193 countries.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Concrete HDI rank shifts show these indices move and matter politically — and India's low rank despite economic scale is a recurring development gap.
insights.quote: The US topped the ranking in 1990, but in 2023 – the most recent dataset – it had fallen to 17, replaced at the top by Iceland, Norway and Switzerland.

insights.claim: The global mean Human Development Index value rose from about 0.61 in 1990 to about 0.76 in 2023, indicating broad long-run improvement in human development across countries.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: A baseline data point for framing development trajectories; the secular HDI rise is the success the NRI wants to extend to nature.
insights.quote: the mean index value of all countries has increased, from ~0.61 in 1990 to ~0.76 in 2023

insights.claim: The author argues that the UN's Sustainable Development Goals failed to gain narrative traction because their sheer abundance of goals and metrics made them hard to distil into a single compelling number.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Explains the design bet that a single simple index beats a sprawling goal set for shaping budgets and reputations.
insights.quote: the abundance of these goals and metrics makes them hard to distil into a simple, compelling metric
insights.locator: What is new in our work is the context.

insights.claim: The author frames degradation of the natural world as a systemic risk to human prosperity, arguing that reducing nature to an exploitable commodity is no longer viable in the 21st century.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Reframes environmental decline as a prosperity/financial-stability risk rather than an ethical externality — the institutional-investor framing.
insights.quote: Degradation of the natural world is a systemic risk to human prosperity

insights.claim: In a globalised economy, environmental damage from consumption is often geographically displaced rather than actually reduced, and the NRI's second dimension is built to make those hidden connections visible.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Core mechanism: 'displacement not reduction' is the trade-and-supply-chain critique that the index operationalizes into a score.
insights.quote: In a globalised economy, environmental damage is often displaced rather than reduced, and this dimension seeks to make those connections visible.

insights.claim: The author notes the three NRI dimensions trade off against each other, for example intensive monoculture can raise food yields and free up land for protected areas while worsening local biodiversity and pollution.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Acknowledges genuine policy trade-offs (intensification vs. land-sparing) that make a single composite score contestable.
insights.quote: intense monoculture (fewer thriving landscapes, increased local pollution) may increase food yields and potentially reduce pressure on agricultural expansion, thus enabling more protected areas
insights.locator: These dimensions can play off each other.

insights.claim: China now accounts for roughly a third of global carbon dioxide emissions, though at much lower per-capita levels than North America.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Per-capita framing reframes the emissions-responsibility debate central to any consumption-based global metric.
insights.quote: China now accounts for about a third of global emissions, albeit at much lower per-capita levels than North America
insights.locator: I first visited China in the early 1990s...

insights.claim: The author asserts China's carbon emissions are set to plateau and decline in coming years as its power and transport systems green and electrify, with EVs increasingly dominating city streets.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: A near-term China emissions peak underpins global decarbonization timelines and EV/clean-power demand theses.
insights.quote: China’s carbon emissions are set to plateau and decline in the coming years as the country’s power and transport systems green and electrify
insights.locator: Still, Hangzhou and many other cities...

insights.claim: The author identifies Hangzhou as the centre of China's AI boom while also reforesting hillslopes and rapidly cleaning its air and water.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Pairs China's AI-industrial growth with active environmental remediation — a counterpoint to the growth-versus-green framing.
insights.quote: increasingly so as the centre of China’s AI boom
insights.locator: I first visited China in the early 1990s...

insights.claim: The author argues environmental discourse is dominated by limit-based concepts (planetary boundaries, carbon budgets, extinction rates) and needs to be balanced by aspirational narratives of what success looks like, not only what to avoid.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Positions the NRI as an aspirational 'flourishing' brand rather than a doom metric — a deliberate communications strategy.
insights.quote: Environmental discourse today is rich in warnings ... they function mainly as limits, telling us what must not be exceeded

insights.claim: The author introduces 'species-loneliness' — human disconnection from the community of fellow species — and argues urban nature access (tree-lined streets, safe parks) makes a city more developed than a concrete metropolis at the same income level.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Coins a memorable concept that elevates urban green infrastructure into a scored development quality.
insights.quote: reduce ‘species-loneliness’, or human disconnection from the community of fellow species

insights.claim: Citing economist Kate Raworth, the author argues that on human co-flourishing with the living world every nation is effectively a 'developing country' with distance still to travel, since none will yet score highly across all dimensions.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Reframes the leaderboard so no country can claim arrival — designed to keep rich nations engaged rather than complacent.
insights.quote: when it comes to reaching a goal of human co-flourishing with the living world, we are all developing countries, and we all have a distance to travel

insights.claim: The author argues that even amid geopolitical fragmentation and strained multilateral institutions, planetary interdependence persists because carbon, biodiversity and water flows ignore borders regardless of political hardening.
insights.speaker: Yadvinder Malhi
insights.so_what: Defends the relevance of a global cooperative metric in a deglobalizing, realpolitik environment — the key adoption risk for the NRI.
insights.quote: Carbon dioxide, biodiversity and water flows ignore borders even when political boundaries harden around them.
insights.locator: We are living through a period of geopolitical tension...
